title: Certificate-Based Authentication
text: Certificate-Based Authentication When TLS is configured for certificate-based authentication, it uses signed certificates to guarantee the identity of communicating peers, and a negotiated master key for encrypting and decrypting the data. The certificate and its associated key make it possible to support a wide range of encryption ciphers. When correctly configured, certificates provide much better security than pre-shared keys. You can create your own certificates or purchase certificates from a Certificate Authority (CA). When creating your own certificates, you can use simple self-signed certificates or you can act as CA yourself and issue certificates for your own use. To enable certificate-based encryption, configure the TLS connector for server verification using the verify_server connector option. Optionally, you can configure client verification using the verify_client connector option. For more information, see Configure the TLS Connector to Use Certificates . Note: All certificates and keys must be in PEM format. Related Links Digital Certificates A digital certificate (also known as a public key certificate) is a digitally-signed document used to validate the identity of the sender in communication between a client and server. Creating Certificates You can use tools such as OpenSSL to create certificates and generate RSA private and public keys for signature signing and verification. A self-signed TLS/SSL certificate is not signed by a publicly trusted certificate authority (CA) but instead by the developer or company that is responsible for the website; as a publicly trusted CA does not sign them, they are usually considered unsafe for public applications and websites. Ciphers Supported by the TLS Connector OpenSSL (which is used by the TLS connector) supports over 135 ciphers that can potentially be used with certificates. verify_client Determines whether a TLS client's certificate will be verified. The default is no. verify_server Determines whether a TLS server's certificate will be verified. The default is no. Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
